key_points: An ecosystem is formed by interactions between living organisms and their environment
Biotic components include plants, animals, and microorganisms
Abiotic components include sunlight, water, air, soil, and temperature
Energy flows through ecosystems mainly from the sun
Producers convert sunlight into food through photosynthesis
Consumers depend on producers or other animals for food
Decomposers break down dead organisms and recycle nutrients
Food webs connect multiple food chains and increase ecosystem stability
examples: Grassland ecosystem: grass, insects, herbivores, predators
Food chain example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox
Aquatic ecosystem: fish, algae, plankton, water and sunlight
Decomposition example: bacteria breaking down dead plants
relationships: Dead organisms → Decomposers → Soil nutrients → Plants
Sunlight → Photosynthesis → Energy for food chains
exam_tips: Draw labeled food chain diagrams when possible
Remember the difference between food chain and food web
Explain roles of producers, consumers, and decomposers clearly
Use real-life ecosystem examples like forest or pond
